§1

Programme snapshot

where programming sits in the 8 semesters

This is the current (2022–2024 revision) B.E. (Biomedical Engineering) scheme at the University College of Engineering, Osmania University — an autonomous college, so its syllabus is self-designed and updated by its own Board of Studies rather than copied from the general OU syllabus. Programming appears as a dedicated subject in Semester II, and its ideas (arrays, structures, files) resurface later inside Signals & Systems, Biomedical Instrumentation, and the MATLAB/LabVIEW-based Virtual Instrumentation Lab.

SemProgramming-relevant courseLanguage / ToolCreditsType
IBasic Mathematics I & II (bridge course, Bi.P.C. entrants)—0 (bridge)Foundational only
IIProgramming for Problem Solving + LabC (on Linux)3 + 1Core
IIComputer Aided Engineering LabCAD software1Core (not language programming)
IVBioinformatics (Professional Elective I)Algorithms, no fixed language mandated3Elective
IVSignals & Systems for BMEConcepts implementable in MATLAB/Python3Core
IVVirtual Instrumentation & Simulation LabMATLAB, LabVIEW1Core
IVBiostatistics (Professional Elective I)"Open source software" (commonly R/Python)3Elective
Reading this table C is the one formally-timetabled programming language in the BME scheme, taught end-to-end in a single semester (Semester II) as ES 201 CS — Programming for Problem Solving, backed by a 2-hour weekly lab (ES 251 CS). Everything else — MATLAB, LabVIEW, "open source software" for statistics — is tool-level, not a taught programming course. Section §3 explains exactly what this means for Python.
§2

Official C syllabus — Semester II

ES 201 CS · verbatim structure from uceou.edu

ES 201 CS — Programming for Problem Solving

Contact hours: 3 lecture/week  |  Evaluation: CIE 40 + SEE 60 = 100 marks  |  Credits: 3

UnitTopics (as published)
IComputer systems & computing environments; computer languages; creating & running programs; software development; flowcharts. Number systems: binary, octal, decimal, hexadecimal. Intro to C — background, structure of a C program, identifiers, data types, variables, constants, I/O statements. Arithmetic operators & expressions — precedence, associativity, type conversions.
IIConditional control: bitwise, relational & logical operators; if, if-else, switch. Loops: for, while, do-while; continue, break, goto. Functions: user-defined functions, inter-function communication, standard functions, parameter passing, recursion. Storage classes: auto, register, static, extern, scope rules, type qualifiers.
IIIPreprocessor commands. Arrays — concept, inter-function communication, 2-D & multi-dimensional arrays, linear & binary search, selection & bubble sort.
IVPointers — inter-function communication, pointer-to-pointer, lvalue/rvalue, arrays & pointers, pointer arithmetic, passing arrays to functions, dynamic memory allocation, array of pointers, pointer to void, pointer to function, command-line arguments. Strings — C strings, string I/O, arrays of strings, string manipulation functions.
VStructures — definition, initialization, nested structures, arrays of structures, structures with functions, pointers to structures, self-referential structures, unions, typedef, enumerated types. File I/O — file modes, streams, standard library I/O functions, character I/O functions.

Textbooks (as prescribed): B.A. Forouzan & R.F. Gilberg, A Structured Programming Approach in C (Cengage); Kernighan & Ritchie, The C Programming Language (2nd ed., PHI); V. Rajaraman, Fundamentals of Computers (PHI); R.G. Dromey, How to Solve it by Computer (Pearson).

ES 251 CS — Programming for Problem Solving Lab

Contact hours: 2 practical/week  |  Evaluation: CIE 25 + SEE 50  |  Credits: 1  |  Done on Linux, using the vi editor and gcc.

Official list of lab programs, in order:

  1. Linux environment orientation: vi editor, compiling & running a program.
  2. Arithmetic, logical, bitwise and ternary operators.
  3. Simple control statements: roots of a quadratic equation; extracting/reversing digits; digit sum; multiplication tables; Armstrong numbers; prime check; magic numbers.
  4. sin(x) and cos(x) via series expansion.
  5. Number-base conversion: binary ⇄ decimal ⇄ octal ⇄ hex.
  6. Pascal's triangle and number pyramids.
  7. Recursion: factorial, Fibonacci, GCD.
  8. Max, min, average, standard deviation of an array.
  9. Reversing an array; removing duplicates.
  10. Matrix addition, multiplication, transpose using functions.
  11. Bubble sort, selection sort.
  12. Linear search and binary search — recursive and iterative.
  13. String manipulation using library functions (strlen, strcat, strcpy...).
  14. String manipulation without library functions.
  15. Counting characters, words, lines in a text file.
  16. File-handling: student memo/report printing.
  17. Linked lists: create, traverse, insert a node, delete a node, reverse the list.

The official syllabus PDF even points students to projecteuler.net for extra practice — that's a genuinely good habit to pick up early.

§3

Where Python actually fits

an honest note, not a marketing one
Transparency check Having gone through the officially published OU/UCE Biomedical Engineering syllabi for Semesters I–IV (the semesters where the college's own scheme places "programming" and "computing" subjects), there is no dedicated, credit-bearing "Python Programming" course in the current BME scheme. The one taught, examined programming language is C (Semester II). Python is not named anywhere in the BME core courses I could verify — it shows up by language-name in the CSE branch's version of "Programming for Problem Solving," not BME's.

So why is Python still worth your time as a BME student? Two honest reasons:

1. It's the natural next step after C

Once you understand variables, loops, functions and arrays in C, Python teaches you nothing new conceptually — it just removes the ceremony (no semicolons, no manual memory management, no declaring types). Employers and research labs in biomedical signal processing, imaging, and bioinformatics overwhelmingly use Python (NumPy, SciPy, Pandas) for exactly the kind of work your Semester IV subjects (Signals & Systems, Biomedical Instrumentation, Bioinformatics elective) describe conceptually.

2. It quietly supports three electives

The Biostatistics elective syllabus explicitly says "Introduction of Open source software and its applications" — in practice this is usually taught with Python or R. The Bioinformatics elective's algorithms (sequence alignment, clustering, neural networks) are almost always prototyped in Python in industry and research. MATLAB, used in the Virtual Instrumentation Lab, and Python are conceptually close enough that Python fluency makes MATLAB easy to pick up too.

What this site does about it Section §5 gives you a genuinely complete, standalone Python course — zero to hero, not just a translation table — because it will make your Semester IV+ electives and your résumé stronger. Treat it as supplementary self-study, and always confirm your own college's current handbook/almanac for the semester you are actually in.
Tutorial track begins
Zero to working programs
§4

C, zero to hero

Cmapped to Unit I–V above, one small step at a time

This is the same "no step assumes anything you haven't just read" treatment as the Python and Data Structures sections. Every stage follows the same pattern: a plain-English idea, a tiny working example, the real output, and a numbered walkthrough of exactly what happened. Type every example yourself on your Linux lab machine — reading code and writing code use different muscles.

0 — Setup: how a C program actually gets from text to a running program

🏗️
Plain-English idea: Python runs your text almost immediately, line by line. C is different — your typed text must first be fully translated into the computer's native machine instructions before anything runs at all. This translation step is called compiling, and it's the single biggest difference between the two languages you're learning on this site.
  • You type your program into a plain text file and save it with a .c extension, e.g. hello.c. In the lab you'll do this with the vi editor.
  • You run gcc hello.c -o hello. gcc is the compiler — the translator. It reads your file, checks it for mistakes, and produces a new file.
  • If there's a mistake (a missing semicolon, a misspelt word), gcc refuses to produce anything and instead prints an error, pointing at the line number.
  • If everything is correct, gcc produces a new file named hello — this is your program, now translated into instructions the machine can execute directly.
  • You run ./hello to actually execute it. The ./ means "look for this program in the current folder."
Compiled vs. interpreted, in one line C is compiled once, ahead of time, into a standalone program you can then run any number of times. Python is interpreted — it's translated and run in one single step, line by line, every time. This is exactly why C programs generally run faster, but Python feels quicker to experiment in.
Try it — lab exercise 1 Type hello.c from stage 1 below, save it, then run exactly these two commands in order: gcc hello.c -o hello and then ./hello. If you see a compiler error instead, read the line number it mentions — it is almost always telling you the truth about where the mistake is.

1 — Anatomy of a C program

💡
Plain-English idea: A computer is an extremely fast, extremely obedient, and extremely literal assistant. It can only do very simple things — add two numbers, compare two numbers, move data from one place to another — but it does billions of them per second, exactly in the order you write them. A C program is nothing more than that exact order of instructions, written in a form gcc can translate.
C#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    printf("Hello, Biomedical Engineer!\n");
    return 0;
}
Hello, Biomedical Engineer!

Pull this apart piece by piece — every single character here has a job:

  • #include <stdio.h> — before compiling your own code, gcc pastes in the contents of the Standard Input/Output header file, because that's where the definition of printf lives. Leave this line out and gcc won't know what printf means.
  • int main() { ... } — every C program must have exactly one function named main. When you run your program, execution always begins at the first line inside main's curly braces.
  • printf(...) — a ready-made function (you didn't write it; stdio.h gave it to you) that prints text to the screen.
  • "Hello, Biomedical Engineer!\n" — text in C must always sit inside double quotes. The \n inside it is not two characters, it's one special instruction meaning "move to a new line."
  • The semicolon ; at the end of printf(...) tells the compiler "this instruction is finished." Forgetting a semicolon is the single most common first-week C error.
  • return 0; — hands control back to the operating system and reports "0 mistakes, finished successfully." A non-zero return value is the traditional way a C program reports that something went wrong.
  • The curly braces { } mark where a block of code starts and ends — everything belonging to main sits between its own opening and closing brace.
Try it yourself Delete the semicolon after the printf line on purpose, save, and compile again. Read the exact wording of the error gcc gives you — learning to read compiler errors calmly, instead of panicking, is half of learning C.

2 — Variables & data types

📦
Plain-English idea: A variable is a labelled box in memory. The label is its name; the box's size and the kind of thing it can hold is its data type. Unlike Python, C makes you state the box's type out loud before you use it — this is called declaring a variable, and the compiler uses it to know exactly how much memory to reserve.
TypeHoldsTypical sizeFormat specifierExample
intwhole numbers4 bytes%dint age = 20;
floatdecimal numbers4 bytes%ffloat heartRate = 72.5;
doubledecimal numbers, more precision8 bytes%lfdouble bp = 120.75;
chara single character1 byte%cchar grade = 'A';
(a string is just char[])text1 byte per character%schar name[20] = "Rao";
C — a simple BMI calculator// A simple BMI calculator
#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    float weightKg = 68.0;
    float heightM  = 1.72;
    float bmi = weightKg / (heightM * heightM);
    printf("BMI = %.2f\n", bmi);
    return 0;
}
BMI = 22.99
  • float weightKg = 68.0; — this single line does two things at once: it declares a box named weightKg big enough for a decimal number, and immediately initialises it with 68.0.
  • The computer reserves exactly 4 bytes for weightKg, another 4 for heightM, and another 4 for bmi — nothing more, nothing less. This is very different from Python, which decides sizes for you behind the scenes.
  • In printf("BMI = %.2f\n", bmi), the %.2f is a format specifier — a placeholder saying "a decimal number goes here, rounded to 2 places after the point." printf then substitutes the value of bmi into that exact spot.
Constants Two ways to make a value that can never change: const float PI = 3.14159; (a typed, checked constant) or #define PI 3.14159 (an older, simpler text-substitution done before compiling — you'll meet this properly in stage 14).
Type casting (float) sum / n forces an integer to be treated as a decimal before the division happens — without it, C would perform whole-number division and silently throw away anything after the decimal point. You'll see exactly this trick again in stage 6.

3 — Operators, all of them

The official syllabus (Unit I–II) explicitly lists arithmetic, relational, logical, bitwise, assignment, and the conditional (ternary) operator — so here they all are, side by side, with a worked value for each.

KindSymbolsExample (a=6, b=4)Result
Arithmetic+ - * / %a % b2 (remainder after 6÷4)
Relational== != > < >= <=a > b1 (C has no true bool in older standards — 1 means true, 0 means false)
Logical&& || !(a>b) && (b>0)1
Bitwise& | ^ ~ << >>a & b4 (works on the raw binary bits, from §4.0's number systems)
Assignment shortcuts+= -= *= /= %=a += 1same as a = a + 1
Increment/decrement++ --a++same as a = a + 1, written after a variable
Ternary (conditional)? :(a>b) ? a : b6 — "if a>b, give a, else give b"
C — bitwise & ternary, from your official lab list#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    int a = 6, b = 4;
    printf("AND: %d\n", a & b);      // 0110 & 0100 = 0100 = 4
    printf("OR:  %d\n", a | b);      // 0110 | 0100 = 0110 = 6
    printf("Larger: %d\n", (a > b) ? a : b);
    return 0;
}
AND: 4 OR: 6 Larger: 6
Common trap = means "store this value" (assignment). == means "are these equal?" (comparison). Writing if (x = 5) by mistake instead of if (x == 5) is legal C, compiles without complaint, and silently does the wrong thing — arguably the single most notorious beginner bug in the whole language.

4 — Input & output, in depth

🎙️
Plain-English idea: printf speaks to the user; scanf listens to the user. Getting scanf right means understanding one new idea early: it needs to know where in memory to place whatever the user types.
C#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    float tempC;
    printf("Enter body temperature (C): ");
    scanf("%f", &tempC);
    printf("You entered: %.1f\n", tempC);
    return 0;
}
  • scanf("%f", &tempC) — the %f tells scanf what kind of value to expect (a decimal number).
  • The & in front of tempC means "the address of" — you are handing scanf the exact memory location of the box named tempC, not the value inside it (which doesn't exist yet).
  • scanf writes the typed value directly into that address. This is your very first glimpse of the idea behind pointers (stage 11) — scanf genuinely cannot do its job without an address to write to.
  • printf, in contrast, never needs & — it only needs to read a value, not write one, so a plain variable name is enough.
A classic trap: reading a string after a number scanf("%s", name) stops reading at the very first space or newline — it cannot read "Aarav Rao" into one variable; it will only capture "Aarav". Also, mixing scanf("%d", ...) followed by scanf("%c", ...) often grabs a leftover newline character instead of what you expect. Both are extremely common early bugs — if your program seems to "skip" an input, this is almost always why.

5 — Decisions: if / else if / else / switch

🚦
Plain-English idea: Real programs need to make choices — "if the patient's temperature is above 37.5°C, flag a fever." if checks a condition; the program only enters the block underneath if that condition works out to true (non-zero).
C — decisions#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    float tempC;
    printf("Enter body temperature (C): ");
    scanf("%f", &tempC);

    if (tempC > 37.5) {
        printf("Fever detected.\n");
    } else if (tempC < 35.0) {
        printf("Hypothermia risk.\n");
    } else {
        printf("Temperature normal.\n");
    }
    return 0;
}

Step-by-step: tracing this by hand for tempC = 36.0

  • Check the first condition: is 36.0 > 37.5? No.
  • Move to else if: is 36.0 < 35.0? No.
  • Both checks failed, so the final else block runs: "Temperature normal." is printed.
  • Only one of the three blocks ever runs for a given input — as soon as one condition matches, C skips straight past the rest.

switch is a cleaner alternative when you're comparing one variable against several exact fixed values (like a menu choice), instead of ranges:

C — switchchar grade = 'B';
switch (grade) {
    case 'A': printf("Excellent\n"); break;
    case 'B': printf("Good\n");      break;
    default:  printf("Needs work\n");
}
Common trap Forget a break; inside a switch and C will keep executing every case below the matching one too ("fall-through") — this is legal C, and it is almost never what a beginner actually wanted.

6 — Loops: for, while, do-while

🔁
Plain-English idea: A loop repeats a block of instructions instead of you copy-pasting it — "record the pulse once every second for 60 seconds" is one line of logic repeated 60 times, not 60 separate lines.
C — for loop: record N pulse readings#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    int n = 5, sum = 0;
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
        int reading;
        printf("Pulse reading %d: ", i);
        scanf("%d", &reading);
        sum += reading;
    }
    printf("Average pulse: %.1f bpm\n", (float)sum / n);
    return 0;
}

A for loop's three parts, taken one at a time

  • Start (i = 1) — runs exactly once, before the loop even begins.
  • Condition to keep going (i <= n) — checked before every single round. The moment this becomes false, the loop stops immediately, without running its body again.
  • What happens after each round (i++) — runs automatically at the end of every round, right before the condition is checked again.
  • So the real order is: start → check condition → run body → i++ → check condition → run body → i++ → ... until the condition fails.
C — while loopint pulse = 60;
while (pulse < 100) {
    printf("%d\n", pulse);
    pulse += 10;   // without this line, the loop would run forever
}
C — do-while loopint choice;
do {
    printf("Enter 1 to continue, 0 to stop: ");
    scanf("%d", &choice);
} while (choice != 0);
LoopUse it when...
foryou know (or can calculate) the exact number of repetitions in advance
whileyou don't know the count in advance — you just keep going while something stays true
do-whilethe loop's body must run at least once, even before the condition is ever checked
break, continue, and goto break exits the loop immediately, skipping everything remaining, even if the condition would still be true. continue skips only the rest of the current round and jumps straight to the condition check. goto jumps to a labelled line anywhere in the function — it's in your official syllabus, but modern C style avoids it almost entirely because it makes code hard to follow; know it for the exam, avoid it in your own code.

7 — Functions

🔧
Plain-English idea: A function is a named, reusable block of instructions — like a lab procedure written once ("how to centrifuge a sample") that anyone can follow by name, feeding in their own sample and getting their own result back.
C — user-defined function#include <stdio.h>

float celsiusToFahrenheit(float c) {
    return (c * 9.0 / 5.0) + 32;
}

int main() {
    float tempC = 37.0;
    printf("%.1f C = %.1f F\n", tempC, celsiusToFahrenheit(tempC));
    return 0;
}
  • float celsiusToFahrenheit(float c) is the function's signature: it returns a float, is named celsiusToFahrenheit, and accepts one float parameter named c.
  • Inside main, calling celsiusToFahrenheit(tempC) copies the value of tempC (37.0) into the function's own local box named c.
  • The function calculates and returns a result — control jumps straight back to exactly where it was called from, carrying that result with it.
The single most important rule about C functions: call by value A function receives a copy of whatever you pass it, never the original variable. If a function changes its own parameter, the caller's variable is completely untouched. This is exactly why scanf needed & in stage 4 — a plain variable couldn't be modified by a function otherwise. Stage 11 (Pointers) shows you the one way around this rule.
Storage classes, briefly (official syllabus, Unit II) auto is the default for any variable inside a function — it exists only while that function is running. static makes a variable keep its value between calls instead of resetting each time. extern lets one file use a variable defined in another file. register is a hint asking the compiler to keep a variable in the CPU's fastest storage; modern compilers mostly ignore it and decide this themselves.

8 — Recursion

🪆
Plain-English idea: Like a set of Russian nesting dolls — to open the big doll you first have to solve the exact same "open the doll" problem on the slightly smaller doll inside it, and so on, until you reach the smallest doll, which just opens directly.
C — recursionint factorial(int n) {
    if (n == 0) return 1;        // base case — stops the recursion
    return n * factorial(n - 1);   // recursive case
}

Step-by-step: tracing factorial(3) by hand

  • factorial(3) isn't 0, so it needs 3 * factorial(2) — but it can't finish this line until it knows what factorial(2) is. It pauses here and waits.
  • factorial(2) similarly needs 2 * factorial(1), and pauses too.
  • factorial(1) needs 1 * factorial(0), and pauses too.
  • factorial(0) finally hits the base case and returns 1 immediately — no more pausing.
  • Now the pauses unwind in reverse: factorial(1) resumes and returns 1 * 1 = 1. factorial(2) resumes and returns 2 * 1 = 2. factorial(3) resumes and returns 3 * 2 = 6.
Why every recursive function needs a base case Every one of those "paused, waiting" calls is secretly sitting on the call stack — the exact same LIFO stack structure from §6.3. If you forget the base case, the function calls itself forever, the stack fills up completely, and the program crashes with a "stack overflow." This is not a coincidence of naming — it's the literal mechanism.

9 — Arrays, the C way

The core idea — a row of numbered lockers with instant access by position — is explained fully with a step-by-step insert/delete walkthrough in §6.1 Arrays. This stage covers only the C-specific syntax on top of that idea.

C — declaring, and a 2-D arrayint pulses[5] = {72, 75, 80, 78, 76};   // a 1-D array, 5 slots
printf("%d\n", pulses[2]);                // 80 — instant, no searching

int readings[3][2] = {              // a 2-D array: 3 patients, 2 readings each
    {72, 75},
    {80, 78},
    {76, 90}
};
printf("%d\n", readings[1][0]);    // 80 — patient index 1, reading index 0
  • int pulses[5] reserves exactly 5 consecutive int-sized boxes — the size must be a fixed number known at compile time; you cannot resize it afterwards, unlike a Python list.
  • A 2-D array is really "an array of arrays" — think of readings as 3 rows, each row itself holding 2 numbers. readings[1][0] means "row 1, column 0."
  • Passing an array to a function (needed for the official matrix-addition/transpose lab exercise) passes only the array's starting address, not a copy of the whole thing — which is why a function can modify the caller's original array, unlike with a single int parameter in stage 7.
C — passing an array to a functionfloat average(int arr[], int n) {
    int sum = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) sum += arr[i];
    return (float) sum / n;
}

10 — Strings, in depth

🔤
Plain-English idea: C has no separate "string" type at all — a string is simply an array of char, with one invisible extra character, '\0' (called the "null terminator"), marking exactly where the text stops.
'A'
'a'
'r'
'a'
'v'
'\0'
C — strings, with the standard library#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>

int main() {
    char patient[50] = "Aarav";
    printf("Patient: %s\n", patient);
    printf("Length: %lu\n", strlen(patient));       // counts chars BEFORE the \0
    strcat(patient, " Rao");                          // appends text onto the end
    printf("After strcat: %s\n", patient);
    return 0;
}
Patient: Aarav Length: 5 After strcat: Aarav Rao

Your official lab explicitly asks you to also do string manipulation without the library — meaning you write your own version of strlen by hand, which is a genuinely excellent exercise for understanding the null terminator:

C — a hand-written strlen()int myStrlen(char str[]) {
    int count = 0;
    while (str[count] != '\0') {   // keep going until the null terminator
        count++;
    }
    return count;
}
Common trap scanf("%s", name) stops reading at the first space, so "Aarav Rao" typed at a prompt only stores "Aarav". To read a whole line including spaces, use fgets(name, 50, stdin) instead.

11 — Pointers, step by step

🏠
Plain-English idea: Every variable lives somewhere in memory, and every memory location has an address — like a house has a street address. A normal variable is the house itself. A pointer is a sticky note with that house's address written on it. If you know the address, you can go to that exact house and change what's inside — even from far away, such as from inside a different function.
C — pointers#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    int pulse = 72;
    int *ptr = &pulse;   // ptr now stores pulse's address

    printf("Value of pulse:        %d\n", pulse);
    printf("Address of pulse:      %p\n", (void*)&pulse);
    printf("Value stored in ptr:   %p\n", (void*)ptr);
    printf("Value ptr points to:   %d\n", *ptr);

    *ptr = 80;   // changes pulse itself, via the pointer
    printf("pulse is now:          %d\n", pulse);
    return 0;
}
Value of pulse: 72 Address of pulse: 0x7ffee2a1c9ac Value stored in ptr: 0x7ffee2a1c9ac Value ptr points to: 72 pulse is now: 80

Step-by-step: what each line actually does

  • int pulse = 72; — an ordinary box, holding 72, sitting at some address (say, 0x7ffee2a1c9ac).
  • int *ptr = &pulse; — &pulse means "give me the address of pulse," not its value. int *ptr declares "ptr is a box whose job is to hold the address of an int." So ptr now contains 0x7ffee2a1c9ac — the address, not 72.
  • *ptr (called "dereferencing") means "go to the address stored in ptr, and give me the value sitting there." That's how printf can print 72 using *ptr.
  • *ptr = 80; means "go to the address stored in ptr, and change the value there to 80" — and because that address is pulse's address, pulse itself becomes 80. Nothing about ptr changed; only what it points to changed.
Why this fixes the call-by-value limit from stage 7 A function can't modify the caller's variable directly — but if you pass a pointer to that variable instead, the function receives the address, and can dereference it to change the original:
C — a function that actually changes the caller's variablevoid addFive(int *p) {
    *p = *p + 5;    // go to the address, change what's there
}

int main() {
    int pulse = 72;
    addFive(&pulse);           // pass the ADDRESS, not the value
    printf("%d\n", pulse);   // 77 — the original really changed
    return 0;
}

Pointers and arrays are close cousins

An array's name, used on its own, already behaves like a pointer to its first element — this is exactly why arrays can be passed to functions "by address" for free, as you saw in stage 9.

C — pointer arithmetic walking through an arrayint pulses[3] = {72, 75, 80};
int *p = pulses;             // p now points at pulses[0]
printf("%d\n", *p);       // 72
p++;                        // move the pointer forward by ONE INT'S WORTH of memory
printf("%d\n", *p);       // 75 — same as pulses[1]
Dynamic memory, briefly (official syllabus) malloc(n * sizeof(int)) asks the operating system for a fresh block of memory big enough for n integers, and hands back a pointer to it — this is exactly how a linked list's nodes (§6.2) get created one at a time, on demand, instead of all at once like an array. Always pair malloc with free(ptr) once you're done with it, or the memory stays reserved forever ("a memory leak"). A pointer to a pointer (int **pp) is simply an address that stores another address — used when a function needs to modify the caller's own pointer, not just the value it points to.

12 — Structures & unions

🗂️
Plain-English idea: A struct is a single patient file folder that bundles several related pieces of information — name, age, pulse — that don't share the same type, so a plain array won't do.
C — structures#include <stdio.h>

struct Patient {
    char name[30];
    int age;
    float pulse;
};

int main() {
    struct Patient p1 = {"Meera Iyer", 34, 76.5};
    printf("%s, age %d, pulse %.1f\n", p1.name, p1.age, p1.pulse);
    return 0;
}
  • struct Patient { ... }; defines the blueprint only — no memory is used yet, exactly like a class definition in Python (§5.14) before you make any objects.
  • struct Patient p1 = {...}; actually creates one real folder, filled in with real values.
  • p1.name, p1.age, p1.pulse — the dot reaches inside the folder to one specific field.

You can also have an array of structures (many patient folders in a row), a pointer to a structure (p1_ptr->age instead of (*p1_ptr).age), and a self-referential structure — a struct that contains a pointer to another struct of the same type. That last one is precisely what a linked-list node (§6.2) is: struct Node { int pulse; struct Node *next; };

C — union: a shared box (uses only the biggest member's worth of memory)union Reading {
    int wholeNumber;
    float decimal;
};
Struct vs. union, in one line A struct gives every field its own separate memory, all present at once. A union makes all its fields share the same memory, so only one of them holds a meaningful value at any given time — used when you want to save space and know only one interpretation matters at once. typedef just lets you give a struct a shorter name (e.g. typedef struct Patient Patient;), and enum lets you name a small fixed set of values (e.g. enum Severity {LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH};) instead of using plain, easily-confused numbers.

13 — File handling, in depth

🗄️
Plain-English idea: Variables disappear the moment your program ends. A file lets your program save data permanently, so it's still there the next time the program runs.
ModeMeaning
"r"Read — the file must already exist
"w"Write — creates the file if missing, erases its old contents if it exists
"a"Append — creates the file if missing, adds new content after whatever's already there
C — writing, then reading a file back#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    FILE *fp = fopen("log.txt", "w");
    fprintf(fp, "Pulse reading: 78 bpm\n");
    fclose(fp);   // ALWAYS close a file — forgetting this can lose unsaved data

    char line[100];
    fp = fopen("log.txt", "r");
    fgets(line, 100, fp);
    printf("Read back: %s", line);
    fclose(fp);
    return 0;
}

Your official lab explicitly asks for a program that counts characters, words, and lines in a text file — here's the pattern, using the character-by-character function fgetc:

C — counting characters, words & lines#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    FILE *fp = fopen("notes.txt", "r");
    int ch, chars = 0, words = 0, lines = 0;
    int insideWord = 0;

    while ((ch = fgetc(fp)) != EOF) {   // EOF = "End Of File", a special signal value
        chars++;
        if (ch == '\n') lines++;
        if (ch == ' ' || ch == '\n') {
            insideWord = 0;
        } else if (insideWord == 0) {
            words++;
            insideWord = 1;
        }
    }
    printf("Characters: %d, Words: %d, Lines: %d\n", chars, words, lines);
    fclose(fp);
    return 0;
}
  • fgetc(fp) reads exactly one character at a time from the file, and returns the special value EOF once there's nothing left — that's how the loop knows when to stop.
  • A "word" is counted only at the exact moment you transition from "inside a space" to "inside actual text" — the insideWord flag is what tracks that transition.
  • A "line" is counted every time a '\n' character is seen.

14 — The preprocessor

✂️
Plain-English idea: Before your code is even compiled, a separate, very literal step called the preprocessor runs through your file doing simple text find-and-replace, based on lines starting with #.
C — preprocessor directives#include <stdio.h>     // paste in stdio.h's contents
#define MAX_PATIENTS 50    // replace every "MAX_PATIENTS" with 50, as plain text
#define SQUARE(x) ((x)*(x)) // a "macro": a tiny reusable text template

int main() {
    int beds[MAX_PATIENTS];
    printf("%d\n", SQUARE(5));   // becomes ((5)*(5)) before compiling, prints 25
    return 0;
}
Why this matters, and where you already used it Every single #include you've written since stage 1 is a preprocessor directive. #define constants (like MAX_PATIENTS) make your code easier to change later — update the number once, at the top, instead of hunting through the whole file for every place a fixed limit was typed by hand.

15 — Capstone project: a Patient Register

This is the same idea as the Python capstone in §5.17 — one program deliberately combining almost everything above (structures, arrays, functions, file handling), so you can see it all work together. It also matches the Weeks 12–14 project suggested in the study plan (§7.1).

C — capstone#include <stdio.h>
#define MAX 50

struct Patient {
    char name[30];
    int pulse;
};

int addPatient(struct Patient list[], int count) {
    printf("Name: ");
    scanf("%s", list[count].name);
    printf("Pulse: ");
    scanf("%d", &list[count].pulse);
    return count + 1;   // the new, updated count of patients
}

void saveToFile(struct Patient list[], int count) {
    FILE *fp = fopen("patients.txt", "w");
    for (int i = 0; i < count; i++) {
        fprintf(fp, "%s %d\n", list[i].name, list[i].pulse);
    }
    fclose(fp);
}

int main() {
    struct Patient patients[MAX];
    int count = 0, choice;

    do {
        printf("\n1. Add patient\n2. Save & exit\nChoice: ");
        scanf("%d", &choice);
        if (choice == 1) {
            count = addPatient(patients, count);
        }
    } while (choice != 2);

    saveToFile(patients, count);
    printf("Saved %d patients to patients.txt\n", count);
    return 0;
}
Build this yourself Type it in, run it, add two or three patients, and open patients.txt afterwards to see it saved. Then try extending it: add a "search by name" option using linear search (§6.8), or rebuild the whole thing using a linked list (§6.2) instead of the fixed-size patients[MAX] array, so it can hold any number of patients.
Deep breath
Now, Python — properly, from the very start
§5

Python: zero to hero

Python18 small stages, each one buildable on its own

Forget C for a moment. Pretend you have never coded anything. This is a complete course, in order — each stage only uses things you already learned in the stages before it. Do not skip ahead; every "hero"-level feature near the end (classes, comprehensions, libraries) leans on the basics being solid.

0 — Setup: where does the code actually run?

You need one thing: a place to type Python and press "run."

  • Easiest, zero-install option: open python.org's browser shell or replit.com and start typing.
  • On your own laptop: install Python from python.org, then open the app called IDLE that comes with it — that's a simple editor + "run" button built for beginners.
  • Two ways to use Python: the interactive shell (type one line, see the result immediately — great for experiments) and a script file ending in .py that you write fully, save, then run all at once (what you'll use for real programs).
Your very first line Open the shell and type print("hello") then press Enter. If you see hello printed back, everything works. That's it — you're now running Python.

1 — Variables & data types

📦
Plain-English idea: A variable is a name you give to a piece of information so you can use that information again later, just by using the name.
Pythonage = 20          # a whole number -> type "int"
height = 1.72     # a decimal number -> type "float"
name = "Aarav"     # text -> type "str" (string)
is_student = True  # True or False -> type "bool"

print(age, height, name, is_student)
print(type(age))    # asks Python: what type is this?
20 1.72 Aarav True <class 'int'>
  • Step 1. When Python sees age = 20, it creates a box in memory containing 20, and labels that box age.
  • Step 2. You never declare a type up front (unlike C's int age;) — Python looks at the value you gave and decides the type automatically. This is called dynamic typing.
  • Step 3. A variable's type can even change later if you reassign it — age = "twenty" would silently turn age into a string. Powerful, but be careful with it.
  • Step 4. Naming rule: variable names can use letters, digits and underscores, but can't start with a digit, and are case-sensitive (Age and age are different variables).
Try it yourself Create three variables for your own name, age, and favourite subject. Print all three in one print() call, separated by commas.

2 — Operators

KindSymbolsExampleResult
Arithmetic+ - * / // % **7 // 2 and 7 % 23 and 1 (// is whole-number division, % is the remainder)
Comparison== != > < >= <=5 > 3True
Logicaland or not(5>3) and (2>1)True
Assignment shortcuts+= -= *= /=x += 1same as x = x + 1
Pythonpulse = 72
pulse += 4          # pulse is now 76
print(pulse > 100 and pulse < 60)   # False — pulse is 76
Common trap = means "store this value" (assignment). == means "are these equal?" (comparison). Mixing them up is the single most common beginner bug in every language, not just Python.

3 — Strings, in depth

🔤
Plain-English idea: A string is just text. In Python you can treat it almost like a mini-array of characters — each character has a position number, starting at 0, just like the array lockers from §6.1.
Pythonname = "Biomedical"
print(name[0])       # 'B'  -> first character, position 0
print(name[-1])      # 'l'  -> -1 always means "last character"
print(name[0:4])     # 'Biom' -> a "slice": positions 0,1,2,3
print(name.upper())  # 'BIOMEDICAL'
print(name.lower())  # 'biomedical'
print(len(name))     # 10 -> number of characters
print(name.replace("Bio", "Neuro"))  # 'Neuromedical'

# joining text and variables cleanly:
age = 20
print(f"{name} student, age {age}")
B l Biom BIOMEDICAL biomedical 10 Neuromedical Biomedical student, age 20
  • Step 1. Indexing (name[0]) gets one character at a known position.
  • Step 2. Slicing (name[0:4]) gets a range of characters — "start at 0, stop before 4," so you get exactly 4 characters.
  • Step 3. Strings have built-in helper actions, called methods, that you call with a dot: .upper(), .lower(), .replace(), and many more.
  • Step 4. An f-string (the f right before the quote) lets you drop a variable straight into a sentence using {curly braces} — much easier than gluing text together with +.

4 — Input & output

Pythonname = input("What is your name? ")
age = int(input("What is your age? "))   # input() always gives text, so we convert it
print(f"Hi {name}, next year you'll be {age + 1}.")
Why int(...)? input() always hands you back text (a string), even if the user typed a number. int("20") converts that text into a real number you can do arithmetic with. Forgetting this is a very common early bug — Python will happily glue "20" + "1" into "201" instead of adding them.

5 — if / elif / else

Pythontemp_c = float(input("Enter body temperature (C): "))

if temp_c > 37.5:
    print("Fever detected.")
elif temp_c < 35.0:
    print("Hypothermia risk.")
else:
    print("Temperature normal.")
The one rule that trips up every beginner Python has no { } to mark a block of code — indentation itself (4 spaces, consistently) is what groups statements together. Everything indented under the if belongs to that if. Get the indentation wrong and the logic changes silently, with no error message.

6 — Loops

Python — for loopfor i in range(5):     # range(5) means 0,1,2,3,4
    print("Reading number", i)
Python — while looppulse = 60
while pulse < 100:
    print(pulse)
    pulse += 10   # without this line, the loop would run forever
  • Step 1. for is for "repeat this a known/countable number of times, or once per item in a collection." range(5) generates the numbers 0 to 4.
  • Step 2. while is for "keep repeating as long as a condition stays true" — you don't need to know the exact count in advance.
  • Step 3. break exits a loop immediately. continue skips straight to the next round without finishing the current one.
  • Step 4. Every while loop needs something inside it that eventually makes the condition false — otherwise it's an infinite loop.

7 — Lists

📝
Plain-English idea: A list is an ordered, growable collection — like a shopping list you can add to, cross items off, or reorder, unlike C's fixed-size array.
Pythonpulses = [72, 75, 80, 78, 76]

print(pulses[0])          # 72 -> first item
pulses.append(90)         # adds 90 to the end
pulses.insert(0, 68)      # inserts 68 at position 0
pulses.remove(80)         # removes the value 80 (first match)
last = pulses.pop()       # removes & returns the last item
pulses.sort()             # sorts the list in place

print(pulses)
print("length:", len(pulses))
print("average:", sum(pulses)/len(pulses))

for p in pulses:            # loop directly over the items — very common in Python
    if p > 90:
        print(p, "is high")
List vs. C array, in one line A Python list can grow and shrink and hold mixed information; a C array is a fixed number of same-type lockers decided up front. Same core idea (§6.1), far fewer rules.

8 — Tuples & sets (two quick, useful cousins of the list)

Python# Tuple: an ordered list that can NEVER change after creation ("immutable")
coordinates = (12.5, 78.3)     # e.g. a fixed sensor location

# Set: an unordered collection with NO duplicates allowed
blood_groups_seen = {"A+", "O+", "A+", "B+"}
print(blood_groups_seen)   # duplicate "A+" is automatically dropped
{'A+', 'O+', 'B+'}

Use a tuple when data shouldn't change (like a fixed coordinate). Use a set when you only care about "which unique things appeared," not order or duplicates.

9 — Dictionaries

🗂️
Plain-English idea: Instead of finding data by position (like list[0]), a dictionary finds data by a meaningful name — like a real patient file folder labelled with fields, not numbers.
Pythonpatient = {
    "name": "Meera Iyer",
    "age": 34,
    "pulse": 76.5
}

print(patient["name"])       # access by key
patient["pulse"] = 80.0       # update a value
patient["ward"] = "ICU"       # add a brand-new field

for key, value in patient.items():
    print(key, "->", value)
Meera Iyer name -> Meera Iyer age -> 34 pulse -> 80.0 ward -> ICU

A dictionary is Python's version of C's struct (§4.7) — a bundle of related fields — except you can add or remove fields any time, and you always access by name ("pulse"), never by position.

10 — Functions

Pythondef celsius_to_fahrenheit(c):
    return (c * 9/5) + 32

def classify_bp(systolic, diastolic=80):   # diastolic has a DEFAULT value
    if systolic < 120:
        return "Normal"
    elif systolic < 140:
        return "Elevated"
    else:
        return "High"

print(celsius_to_fahrenheit(37))
print(classify_bp(125))            # uses default diastolic=80
print(classify_bp(125, 95))        # overrides the default
98.6 Elevated Elevated
  • Step 1. def name(parameters): starts a function definition; everything indented beneath it is the function's body.
  • Step 2. A parameter with = something (like diastolic=80) is optional — if the caller doesn't supply it, the default is used.
  • Step 3. return sends a value back to wherever the function was called from, and ends the function immediately.
  • Step 4. A variable created inside a function only exists inside that function — this is called scope. It disappears once the function finishes.

11 — Modules & the standard library

📚
Plain-English idea: A module is a file of ready-made functions someone else already wrote, so you don't reinvent square roots or random numbers. Python ships with a huge built-in collection called the standard library.
Pythonimport math
import random
from datetime import date

print(math.sqrt(144))          # 12.0
print(random.randint(60, 100))  # a random pulse-like number between 60 and 100
print(date.today())             # today's date

import math loads the whole module; you then use math.sqrt(...). from datetime import date loads just one specific tool out of a bigger module, so you can call it directly as date instead of datetime.date.

12 — Handling errors, on purpose

🛟
Plain-English idea: Sometimes something goes wrong that you can predict — a user types letters instead of a number, or a file doesn't exist. Instead of letting the whole program crash, you can catch the problem and respond calmly.
Pythontry:
    age = int(input("Enter age: "))
    print("Age in 5 years:", age + 5)
except ValueError:
    print("That wasn't a valid whole number. Please try again.")
finally:
    print("Input attempt finished.")

try: holds code that might fail. except ValueError: only runs if that specific kind of error happens (here: converting bad text to a number). finally: always runs, whether there was an error or not — useful for clean-up steps.

13 — Files

Python — writing, then reading, a filewith open("log.txt", "w") as f:   # "w" = write (overwrites the file)
    f.write("Pulse reading: 78 bpm\n")
    f.write("Pulse reading: 82 bpm\n")

with open("log.txt", "r") as f:   # "r" = read
    for line in f:
        print("Read:", line.strip())

with open("log.txt", "a") as f:   # "a" = append, adds to the end
    f.write("Pulse reading: 90 bpm\n")

with open(...) as f: automatically closes the file for you when the indented block ends — C makes you remember fclose() yourself, and forgetting it is a classic bug. line.strip() removes the invisible newline character from the end of each line.

14 — Classes & objects (this is the "hero" step)

🧬
Plain-English idea: So far, a dictionary bundled data (name, age, pulse) but had no behaviour. A class is a blueprint that bundles data and the actions that belong with it, together, into one reusable design. Each patient you create from the blueprint is called an object (or "instance").
Python — a Patient blueprintclass Patient:
    def __init__(self, name, age, pulse):   # runs automatically when a new Patient is made
        self.name = name
        self.age = age
        self.pulse = pulse

    def is_tachycardic(self):        # a behaviour that belongs to every Patient
        return self.pulse > 100

    def describe(self):
        status = "high pulse" if self.is_tachycardic() else "normal pulse"
        return f"{self.name} ({self.age}): {self.pulse} bpm, {status}"

# creating two separate objects from the same blueprint:
p1 = Patient("Meera Iyer", 34, 76)
p2 = Patient("Rohan Das", 45, 112)

print(p1.describe())
print(p2.describe())
Meera Iyer (34): 76 bpm, normal pulse Rohan Das (45): 112 bpm, high pulse
  • Step 1. class Patient: defines the blueprint's name.
  • Step 2. __init__ is a special function that runs the moment you create a new object — it's where you set up that object's own data.
  • Step 3. self means "this particular object" — it's how describe() knows whether to talk about p1 or p2.
  • Step 4. p1 and p2 are two independent objects built from the same blueprint — changing p1.pulse never affects p2.
  • Step 5. (a peek further) A class can also inherit from another class to reuse and extend a blueprint, e.g. class ICUPatient(Patient): would automatically get everything Patient has, plus anything new you add.
Why this matters for you Everything you already learned — variables, functions, dictionaries — was secretly preparing you for this. A class is just "a dictionary of data, plus functions that know how to work on that data, packaged together." This is how almost all real-world Python software (including biomedical signal-processing libraries) is organised.

15 — Comprehensions & lambda (writing Python more fluently)

Pythonpulses = [72, 110, 65, 130, 88]

# the long way, using a loop:
high = []
for p in pulses:
    if p > 100:
        high.append(p)

# the same result, as a "list comprehension" — one readable line:
high = [p for p in pulses if p > 100]
print(high)

# lambda: a tiny, unnamed, throwaway function, often used inline
squared = list(map(lambda x: x * x, [1, 2, 3]))
print(squared)
[110, 130] [1, 4, 9]

A list comprehension is not a new concept — it's the exact same loop-and-filter idea from §6–7, just written compactly. Learn the long way first (you just did); reach for comprehensions once the long way feels automatic.

16 — A taste of NumPy, Matplotlib & Pandas

These are not part of standard Python — they're separate, extremely popular packages you install once with pip install numpy matplotlib pandas. They're what actually gets used in signal-processing and biomedical data work, so it's worth seeing them once.

Python — NumPy: fast arrays of numbersimport numpy as np

ecg_sample = np.array([0.1, 0.4, 1.2, 0.3, -0.2, 0.1])
print("mean:", ecg_sample.mean())
print("max:", ecg_sample.max())
print("above 0.3:", ecg_sample[ecg_sample > 0.3])
Python — Matplotlib: plotting a signalimport matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.plot(ecg_sample)
plt.title("Sample ECG-like signal")
plt.xlabel("Time step")
plt.ylabel("Voltage (mV)")
plt.show()
Python — Pandas: tables of data (like a spreadsheet)import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("patients.csv")
print(df.head())              # first 5 rows
print(df["pulse"].mean())   # average of the "pulse" column
You already know enough to read this NumPy arrays are lists with superpowers, Matplotlib calls are just functions (§10) you feed data into, and a Pandas DataFrame is basically a big table of dictionaries (§9). Nothing here is a new concept — only new vocabulary on top of ideas you already have.

17 — Capstone project: a Patient Vitals Tracker

This one program deliberately uses almost everything above — variables, loops, functions, a dictionary/class, file handling, and error handling — so you can see them work together.

Python — capstoneclass Patient:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
        self.readings = []          # a list living inside an object

    def add_reading(self, pulse):
        self.readings.append(pulse)

    def average(self):
        if not self.readings:
            return 0
        return sum(self.readings) / len(self.readings)

    def flagged(self):
        return [p for p in self.readings if p > 100 or p < 50]

def main():
    patient = Patient(input("Patient name: "))

    while True:
        raw = input("Enter a pulse reading (or 'done'): ")
        if raw.lower() == "done":
            break
        try:
            patient.add_reading(int(raw))
        except ValueError:
            print("Please enter a whole number, or 'done'.")

    print(f"\nAverage pulse for {patient.name}: {patient.average():.1f} bpm")
    print("Flagged (abnormal) readings:", patient.flagged())

    with open("vitals_log.txt", "a") as f:
        f.write(f"{patient.name}: avg={patient.average():.1f}, flagged={patient.flagged()}\n")
    print("Saved to vitals_log.txt")

main()
Build this yourself Type it in, run it, break it on purpose (type letters instead of numbers, type nothing), and watch how each part you learned earlier is the reason the program doesn't crash. Then try extending it: add a second patient, or compute the highest and lowest reading too.
The main event
Data Structures — the smallest possible steps
§6

Data Structures, one small step at a time

Conceptno step assumes anything you haven't just read

A data structure is just a way of arranging information so a particular job — finding something, adding something, removing something, keeping things in order — is fast and doesn't waste space. Nothing below requires cleverness. Every idea is a real-world picture first, a tiny numbered walkthrough second, and code last.

6.0 — Before anything else: what is "memory," really?

🏨
Real-world picture: Imagine your computer's memory (RAM) as one enormous hotel corridor, lined with millions of identical numbered rooms. Every single value your program ever creates — a number, a letter, anything — gets placed into one of these rooms. The room number is called an address. A data structure is simply a strategy for which rooms you use and how you connect them.
  • An array's strategy: book a block of consecutive rooms, all the same size.
  • A linked list's strategy: book rooms wherever there's space, and leave a note in each room saying which room to visit next.

Keep this hotel picture in mind — every structure below is just a different room-booking strategy, chosen to make one particular job fast.

6.1 — Arrays: the row of lockers

🗄️
Real-world picture: A row of identical, numbered lockers bolted to a wall, side by side, no gaps. Locker 0, locker 1, locker 2 ... each holding exactly one item.

Why numbering starts at 0, not 1

Think of the locker number as "how many steps to walk from the very first locker." The first locker itself needs 0 steps to reach — so it's locker number 0. This single habit (counting from 0) is one of the first things that feels odd and then becomes automatic.

Step-by-step: reading an item

  • You ask for locker[2].
  • The computer already knows exactly where locker 0 begins, and exactly how wide one locker is.
  • It calculates: start position + (2 × locker width) — simple multiplication, done instantly.
  • It jumps straight there. It never had to look inside locker 0 or locker 1 first.

This "jump straight there" behaviour is called random access, and it's an array's single biggest strength: reading arr[47] costs the same tiny effort whether the array has 100 items or 10,000,000.

72
[0]
75
[1]
80
[2]
78
[3]
76
[4]

Step-by-step: inserting a new value in the middle

Say you want to insert 74 at position 2 of [72, 75, 80, 78, 76].

  • There is no empty locker at position 2 — every locker is already bolted down and occupied.
  • So first, the value in locker 4 (76) is copied one locker to the right, into a new locker 5.
  • Then the value in locker 3 (78) is copied into locker 4.
  • Then the value in locker 2 (80) is copied into locker 3.
  • Only now is locker 2 empty, and 74 can finally be placed there.

Notice how much shifting happened just to insert one value. That's the array's one real weakness — inserting or deleting anywhere except the very end is slow, because everything after it must physically shift. In C, there's a second limitation too: you must decide the total number of lockers up front and can't change it later (Python's list quietly handles this resizing for you, as you saw in §7 of the Python course).

Cint pulses[5] = {72, 75, 80, 78, 76};
printf("%d\n", pulses[2]);   // prints 80 — instantly, no searching
Pythonpulses = [72, 75, 80, 78, 76]
print(pulses[2])    # prints 80 — Python lists auto-resize, unlike C arrays
OperationHow fast, in plain words
Read item at a known positionInstant, regardless of array size
Insert/delete in the middleSlow — everything after must shift
Insert/delete at the very endFast (if there's spare room)

6.2 — Linked lists: the treasure hunt

🔗
Real-world picture: A treasure hunt. Clue #1, found anywhere, says "the next clue is under the park bench." You walk there, find clue #2, which says "the next clue is on the library's third shelf." The clues themselves can be scattered anywhere in the building — only the notes connecting them matter.

What one "clue" (node) actually contains

Each node holds exactly two things: (1) the actual data, and (2) a pointer — the "next clue" — telling you where the following node lives in memory. There's no requirement that nodes sit next to each other, unlike array lockers.

72
→
75
→
80
→
NULL

The very last node's "next" points to NULL — a special value meaning "there is nothing after this; the chain ends here."

Step-by-step: inserting 74 at the front

  • A brand new node is created somewhere in memory, containing 74.
  • That new node's "next" pointer is set to point at the current first node (the one holding 72).
  • The list's official starting point ("head") is updated to point at the new node instead.
  • Nothing else in the whole list had to move even slightly — unlike the array case above.

Step-by-step: finding the 3rd item

  • Start at the head (first node).
  • Follow its "next" pointer to reach node 2.
  • Follow node 2's "next" pointer to reach node 3.
  • There is no way to "jump straight to node 3" the way arrays can jump to locker 3 — you must walk the chain one link at a time.

This is the trade-off, in one sentence: linked lists make inserting/deleting cheap, but make reaching a specific position slow — exactly the opposite trade-off from arrays.

C — a linked list node, and inserting at the frontstruct Node {
    int pulse;
    struct Node *next;
};

struct Node* insertFront(struct Node* head, int value) {
    struct Node* newNode = (struct Node*) malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    newNode->pulse = value;
    newNode->next  = head;   // new node points to the old first node
    return newNode;      // new node is now the head (start) of the list
}
Python — the same idea, built by handclass Node:
    def __init__(self, pulse):
        self.pulse = pulse
        self.next = None     # None is Python's NULL

def insert_front(head, value):
    new_node = Node(value)
    new_node.next = head
    return new_node

# Python's own built-in list already behaves like a flexible array (§6.1's Python
# side), so hand-built linked lists here are mostly taught so you truly understand
# the mechanism, not because you'd reach for them daily.

6.3 — Stacks: the plate dispenser

🍽️
Real-world picture: A canteen's spring-loaded plate dispenser. You can only take the top plate. You can only add a new plate to the top. The plate placed first, at the very bottom, is the last one anyone will ever reach.

This rule has a name: LIFO — Last In, First Out. There are only ever two moves.

  • push — place a new item on top.
  • pop — remove and return whatever is currently on top.

Step-by-step: push 72, push 75, push 80, then pop twice

  • push 72 → stack is [72], top = 72
  • push 75 → stack is [72, 75], top = 75
  • push 80 → stack is [72, 75, 80], top = 80
  • pop → returns 80, stack is now [72, 75]
  • pop → returns 75, stack is now [72]

Notice 72 — the very first thing pushed — is now the hardest to reach; it's at the bottom, exactly like the plate dispenser.

top →
3rd pushed
2nd pushed
1st pushed (bottom)

Where you've already met this without knowing it: your browser's Back button is a stack of pages you visited. When a C function calls another function (§4.4), the computer secretly uses a stack to remember where to return to — which is exactly why infinite recursion with no base case crashes with a "stack overflow": the stack of "remember to return here" notes grows forever until memory runs out.

C — array-based stack#define MAX 100
int stack[MAX], top = -1;

void push(int value) { stack[++top] = value; }
int  pop()             { return stack[top--]; }
Python — a Python list already IS a stackstack = []
stack.append(72)    # push
stack.append(75)    # push
stack.append(80)    # push
last = stack.pop()   # pop -> removes and returns 80
print(stack, last)
[72, 75] 80

6.4 — Queues: the hospital token counter

🎫
Real-world picture: The token queue at a hospital's OPD counter. Whoever took the earliest token is served first. New arrivals join the back of the line. Nobody can cut in.

This rule has a name: FIFO — First In, First Out. Again, only two moves.

  • enqueue — join the back of the line.
  • dequeue — the person at the front leaves.

Step-by-step: three patients arrive, then two are served

  • enqueue Patient A → line is [A]
  • enqueue Patient B → line is [A, B]
  • enqueue Patient C → line is [A, B, C]
  • dequeue → A is served and leaves, line is now [B, C]
  • dequeue → B is served and leaves, line is now [C]

Compare this directly with the stack above: same two ideas (add one end, remove one end), but a queue removes from the opposite end it adds to, while a stack removes from the same end it adds to. That single difference is the entire distinction between FIFO and LIFO.

front →
B
C
← back (new patients join here) 
Python — using the recommended deque (double-ended queue)from collections import deque
queue = deque()
queue.append("Patient A")    # enqueue
queue.append("Patient B")    # enqueue
queue.append("Patient C")    # enqueue
next_patient = queue.popleft()   # dequeue -> "Patient A"
print(next_patient, list(queue))
Patient A ['Patient B', 'Patient C']

6.5 — Trees: the hospital's organisation chart

🌳
Real-world picture: A hospital's organisation chart: the Medical Superintendent at the top, Heads of Department reporting to them, and doctors reporting to each Head. Information branches outward and never loops back on itself.

Vocabulary, in plain terms: the topmost box (Medical Superintendent) is the root. Each box is a node. A box directly below another is its child. A box with no boxes below it is a leaf.

A special, very common tree: the Binary Search Tree (BST)

A BST adds one strict rule: each node can have at most two children, and for every node — everything smaller goes to the left, everything bigger goes to the right.

50 (root)
30
70
20
40
60
80

Step-by-step: searching for 60 in the tree above

  • Start at the root, 50. Is 60 bigger or smaller than 50? Bigger → go right.
  • Now at 70. Is 60 bigger or smaller than 70? Smaller → go left.
  • Now at 60. Match found — stop.

Just three steps, in a tree holding 7 values. Each comparison threw away an entire half of the remaining possibilities — a bit like a well-organised patient-record cabinet split first into "A–M" and "N–Z," which instantly halves your search space.

C — a BST nodestruct TreeNode {
    int value;
    struct TreeNode *left, *right;
};
Where BME meets this File-system folders, a decision tree for diagnosing symptoms, and hierarchical clustering of gene-expression data (mentioned in your Bioinformatics elective) are all trees in disguise.

6.6 — Graphs: the referral network

🗺️
Real-world picture: A network of hospitals that refer patients to each other for specialised treatment. Hospital A might refer to both B and C; B might also refer to C, or even back to A. Unlike a tree, there's no single "top," and connections can loop back around.

A graph is simply a set of nodes ("vertices," e.g. hospitals) connected by edges (e.g. referral links), with no restriction on how they connect. Every tree you just saw in §6.5 is actually a special, more restricted kind of graph — one with no loops and exactly one path between any two nodes. Google Maps' road network, a social network's friendships, and neuron-to-neuron connections are all graphs.

6.7 — Sorting: organising patient files by name

🗃️
Real-world picture: A stack of patient files dumped in random order that you need to arrange alphabetically before filing them.

Bubble sort, walked through one pass at a time

Starting list: [80, 72, 78, 75]. The rule: look at each neighbouring pair, left to right; if the left one is bigger, swap them.

  • Compare 80 and 72 → 80 is bigger, swap → [72, 80, 78, 75]
  • Compare 80 and 78 → 80 is bigger, swap → [72, 78, 80, 75]
  • Compare 80 and 75 → 80 is bigger, swap → [72, 78, 75, 80]
  • End of pass 1. Notice 80 — the biggest value — has "bubbled" all the way to the end.
  • Pass 2: compare 72&78 (no swap), compare 78&75 → swap → [72, 75, 78, 80]
  • Pass 3: compare 72&75 (no swap needed) → the list is now fully sorted.
C — bubble sortvoid bubbleSort(int arr[], int n) {
    for (int i = 0; i < n-1; i++)
        for (int j = 0; j < n-1-i; j++)
            if (arr[j] > arr[j+1]) {
                int temp = arr[j];
                arr[j] = arr[j+1];
                arr[j+1] = temp;
            }
}

Selection sort, walked through the same way

Same starting list: [80, 72, 78, 75]. The rule: find the smallest value in the unsorted part, and swap it into the front of that unsorted part.

  • Scan the whole list for the smallest value → 72 (at position 1). Swap it with position 0 → [72, 80, 78, 75]. Position 0 is now finalised.
  • Scan positions 1–3 for the smallest → 75 (at position 3). Swap it with position 1 → [72, 75, 78, 80]. Position 1 is now finalised.
  • Scan positions 2–3 for the smallest → 78 is already smaller than 80, no swap needed. Position 2 is finalised, and position 3 is automatically correct too.
Python — both ideasdef bubble_sort(arr):
    n = len(arr)
    for i in range(n-1):
        for j in range(n-1-i):
            if arr[j] > arr[j+1]:
                arr[j], arr[j+1] = arr[j+1], arr[j]   # Python can swap in one line
    return arr

def selection_sort(arr):
    n = len(arr)
    for i in range(n):
        smallest = i
        for j in range(i+1, n):
            if arr[j] < arr[smallest]:
                smallest = j
        arr[i], arr[smallest] = arr[smallest], arr[i]
    return arr

# in real projects, Python's built-in sorted(arr) is used instead — but knowing
# HOW sorting works underneath is exactly what your syllabus is testing.
Why "how fast" matters Both methods above do roughly n × n comparisons for n files: 10 files ≈ 100 comparisons, but 10,000 files ≈ 100,000,000. That's why large real-world software uses cleverer algorithms — but these simple ones are exactly right for learning the underlying idea, which is what the exam and viva actually test.

6.8 — Searching: finding one file in the cabinet

🔎
Real-world picture: Looking for one specific patient's file.

Linear search, step by step

Looking for 78 in [72, 75, 80, 78, 76] — files in random order, not sorted:

  • Check position 0: 72. Not a match. Move on.
  • Check position 1: 75. Not a match. Move on.
  • Check position 2: 80. Not a match. Move on.
  • Check position 3: 78. Match! Stop — return position 3.

Works on any pile, sorted or not — but in the worst case (the value is last, or missing entirely), you check every single item.

Binary search, step by step (needs a sorted list first)

Looking for 78 in the already-sorted list [72, 75, 76, 78, 80] (positions 0 to 4):

  • low = 0, high = 4. Middle position = (0+4)/2 = 2, value there = 76. Is 78 bigger or smaller than 76? Bigger → the answer must be in the right half, so narrow: low = 3.
  • Now low = 3, high = 4. Middle position = (3+4)/2 = 3, value there = 78. Match! Stop.

Just two checks, instead of four. Every single check throws away half of whatever's left — which is why it gets dramatically faster than linear search as the pile grows, provided the pile is sorted first.

72
75
76
78
80
C — binary searchint binarySearch(int arr[], int n, int target) {
    int low = 0, high = n - 1;
    while (low <= high) {
        int mid = (low + high) / 2;
        if (arr[mid] == target) return mid;
        else if (arr[mid] < target) low  = mid + 1;
        else                      high = mid - 1;
    }
    return -1;   // not found
}
Search on 1,000,000 sorted itemsRoughly how many checks
Linear search (worst case)1,000,000
Binary search (worst case)~20
A quick, plain-English word on "Big-O" (why programmers say O(n) and O(log n))

You'll hear this notation constantly once you go past your first year. It simply describes how the amount of work grows as the amount of data grows, ignoring exact numbers:

  • O(1) — constant. Reading arr[47]: same speed no matter the array size.
  • O(log n) — grows very slowly. Binary search: doubling the data adds just one more check.
  • O(n) — grows in direct proportion. Linear search: doubling the data doubles the work.
  • O(n²) — grows with the square. Bubble/selection sort: doubling the data quadruples the work.

6.9 — Cheat sheet: all eight, side by side

StructureReal-world pictureBest atWeak at
ArrayRow of numbered lockersInstant read by positionInsert/delete in the middle
Linked listTreasure hunt cluesCheap insert/delete anywhereReaching a specific position
StackPlate dispenser (LIFO)Undo actions, matching bracketsReaching anything but the top
QueueHospital token line (FIFO)Fair, ordered processingReaching anything but the front
Tree (BST)Org chart / sorted cabinetFast search when sortedCan get "unbalanced" and slow
GraphHospital referral networkModelling any real connectionsCan get complex to search fully
SortingAlphabetising filesMaking later searches fastSimple methods are slow on huge piles
SearchingFinding one fileBinary search is very fast if sortedNeeds sorted data first
Putting it into a schedule
Semester I → VIII
§7

Study plan

a realistic semester-by-semester map

7.1 — Semester II: your 14-week C roadmap (mapped to the official units)

Weeks 1–2 · Unit I

Foundations

Number systems, C program structure, data types, printf/scanf, arithmetic operators. Do: write 5 tiny programs a day (temperature converter, area calculators, simple arithmetic). Get comfortable compiling on Linux with gcc.

Weeks 3–5 · Unit II

Decisions, loops, functions

if/else, switch, all three loop types, then functions and recursion. Do: every "simple control statement" exercise from your official lab list (quadratic roots, Armstrong numbers, prime check, magic numbers) — these are exam favourites, not busywork.

Weeks 6–8 · Unit III

Arrays & your first algorithms

1-D and 2-D arrays, then bubble sort, selection sort, linear & binary search (§6.7–6.8 above). Do: matrix addition/multiplication/transpose using functions — this combines arrays and functions, a favourite viva question.

Weeks 9–11 · Unit IV

Pointers & strings

The hardest conceptual jump (§4.6) — budget real time here, don't rush it. Do: re-implement 2–3 earlier array programs using pointer arithmetic instead of indexing, to feel the equivalence.

Weeks 12–14 · Unit V

Structures, unions, files, linked lists

Do: build one small combined project — e.g. a "Patient Register" that stores several struct Patient records in a linked list, lets you add/search/delete a patient, and saves the list to a file on exit and reloads it on start-up. This one project touches almost every official Unit IV–V lab exercise at once.

7.2 — A parallel, self-paced 12-week Python zero-to-hero + DSA track

Weeks 1–2 · §5 stages 0–6

Real basics

Setup, variables, operators, strings, input/output, if/elif/else, loops. Translate 5 of your existing C programs into Python, one concept at a time.

Weeks 3–4 · §5 stages 7–9

Collections

Lists, tuples, sets, dictionaries. Re-solve your string-manipulation lab exercises without string.h, using Python's built-in string methods instead.

Weeks 5–6 · §5 stages 10–13

Structure your code

Functions, modules, error handling, files. Rebuild your "count characters/words/lines" C lab program in Python, with proper try/except around file reading.

Week 7 · §5 stage 14

OOP

Classes and objects. Turn your C struct Patient into a Python class Patient with real behaviour (methods), not just data.

Weeks 8–9 · §6.1–6.4

Core data structures by hand

Implement a stack, a queue, and a singly linked list from scratch in Python even though Python gives you shortcuts — the goal is understanding the mechanism, not convenience.

Week 10 · §6.5, 6.7–6.8

Trees & algorithms

Build a simple Binary Search Tree; implement bubble sort, selection sort, linear and binary search, then compare against Python's built-in sorted().

Weeks 11–12 · §5.16–5.17

A biomedical mini-project

Load a small CSV of simulated ECG or pulse readings with Pandas, compute average/min/max, flag abnormal readings with a list comprehension, and plot them with Matplotlib — a genuinely useful, portfolio-worthy project that also previews your Semester IV Signals & Systems course.

7.3 — General exam & viva habits that consistently work

For CIE / SEE (theory)

  • Redraw every diagram in this guide by hand from memory — don't just re-read it.
  • For each concept, write its one-sentence, plain-English definition in your own words before checking this page's version.
  • Practice tracing recursive functions, pointer code, and sort/search algorithms line-by-line on paper before typing anything — exactly like the numbered "step-by-step" walkthroughs above.

For the lab exam / viva

  • Type every program from memory at least once — don't just read the solution.
  • Be ready to explain, out loud, why each line exists, not just what it does.
  • Know the difference between a compile-time error, a run-time error, and a logical error, with an example of each from your own code.
§8

Resources & textbooks

as prescribed, plus a few honest extras

Official C syllabus references

  • B.A. Forouzan & R.F. Gilberg — A Structured Programming Approach in C (Cengage, 2007)
  • Brian W. Kernighan & Dennis M. Ritchie — The C Programming Language, 2nd ed. (PHI, 2006)
  • V. Rajaraman — The Fundamentals of Computers, 4th ed. (PHI, 2006)
  • R.G. Dromey — How to Solve it by Computer (Pearson, 2006)
  • Practice: projecteuler.net (linked directly in the official lab manual)

Good, free next steps for Python + DSA

  • Allen B. Downey — Think Python (O'Reilly) — free, gentle, used elsewhere in OU's own AICTE model curriculum
  • Ellis Horowitz & Sartaj Sahni — Fundamentals of Data Structures — the standard reference cited in OU's own CSE data-structures syllabus
  • Official Python docs tutorial — docs.python.org/3/tutorial
  • Practice: revisit projecteuler.net in Python once you've finished the C track — solving the same problems twice cements both languages
Always double-check Syllabi at autonomous colleges are revised roughly every 1–2 years by the Board of Studies. Before an exam, cross-check this page's Unit breakdown against your own department's current handout or the latest PDF on uceou.edu/dept-bme.php — this page reflects the AY 2022–23 (Sem I–II) and AY 2023–24 (Sem III–IV) published versions.
Built as a study reference for B.E. Biomedical Engineering, University College of Engineering, Osmania University. Syllabus content sourced from official PDFs published at uceou.edu (Department of Biomedical Engineering). Not an official university publication — always confirm against your current department handout.
